The user is asking for a biography of Eric Hacker. Let me search for information about this person. The name could refer to different people, so I need to find out who Eric Hacker is - he might be a baseball player, academic, or someone else notable.Based on my research, the most prominent person named "Eric Hacker" is the American former professional baseball pitcher. Here is his biography:

---

## Biography of Eric Hacker

**Eric Lynn Hacker** (born March 26, 1983, in Duncanville, Texas) is an American former professional baseball pitcher. He stands 6 feet 1 inch tall and weighs 230 pounds. He throws right-handed and is a switch hitter.

### Early Life and High School
Hacker grew up in Duncanville, Texas, and attended Duncanville High School. He was named the starting quarterback on the school's football team his senior year but broke his foot in the second game of the season. Although some colleges were interested in him as a football player, the injury led him to concentrate on baseball. As a senior, he earned third-team Class 5A All-State honors as a pitcher.

### Professional Career

**New York Yankees (2002√¢¬Ä¬ì2009):** Hacker was drafted by the New York Yankees in the 23rd round (696th overall) of the 2002 MLB Draft. He pitched in their minor league system from 2002 to 2009, though he missed the entire 2004 season after undergoing Tommy John surgery and the entire 2006 season after right shoulder surgery. His best minor league season in the Yankees organization came in 2008, when he posted a combined 9√¢¬Ä¬ì6 record with a 2.43 ERA across High-A and Double-A.

**Pittsburgh Pirates (2009):** Hacker was traded to the Pirates on May 16, 2009, in exchange for R√É¬≥mulo S√É¬°nchez. He made his MLB debut on September 22, 2009, and appeared in three games in relief that season, posting a 6.00 ERA.

**San Francisco Giants (first stint, 2010):** He signed with the Giants in December 2009 and spent the entire 2010 season with the Triple-A Fresno Grizzlies, where he led the Pacific Coast League with 16 wins√¢¬Ä¬îthe highest total in the league since 1995.

**Minnesota Twins (2011):** Hacker appeared in two games for the Twins in April 2011 but spent most of the season with Triple-A Rochester, where he struggled to a 7√¢¬Ä¬ì14 record and 6.10 ERA.

**San Francisco Giants (second stint, 2012):** Re-signed with the Giants, he made his first career MLB start on April 27, 2012. He appeared in four total games (one start) for San Francisco, going 0√¢¬Ä¬ì1 with a 5.59 ERA. With Fresno, he was selected to the PCL All-Star Game and posted a 12√¢¬Ä¬ì6 record.

**NC Dinos (2013√¢¬Ä¬ì2017):** Hacker signed with the NC Dinos of the KBO League for their inaugural 2013 season. His best season came in 2015, when he led the KBO with 19 wins alongside a 3.13 ERA and 164 strikeouts, earning the pitcher's Golden Glove Award. He pitched in the 2016 Korean Series and won the deciding fifth game of the 2017 Semi-Playoffs against the Lotte Giants.

**Nexen Heroes (2018):** Hacker signed a $300,000 contract with the Nexen Heroes in June 2018.

Over his six seasons in the KBO League, Hacker compiled a 61√¢¬Ä¬ì37 record (.622 winning percentage) with a 3.66 ERA and 675 strikeouts.

### Pitching Style
Hacker threw four pitches: a fastball, a slider, a curveball, and a changeup. Early in his career he relied primarily on the fastball and slider, developing the curveball and changeup later.

### Personal Life
Hacker lives in Southlake, Texas, with his wife Christine, whom he married in November 2008. He is a Dallas Cowboys fan and enjoys fishing and pool.

---

**Sources:** Wikipedia articles on "Eric Hacker" (the baseball player) and "Eric Hacker (para-athlete)"; Baseball-Reference.com career statistics.

FINAL ANSWER: Eric Lynn Hacker (born March 26, 1983, in Duncanville, Texas) is an American former professional baseball pitcher who played in Major League Baseball for the Pittsburgh Pirates (2009), Minnesota Twins (2011), and San Francisco Giants (2012), and in the KBO League for the NC Dinos (2013√¢¬Ä¬ì2017) and Nexen Heroes (2018). Drafted by the New York Yankees in the 23rd round of the 2002 MLB Draft, he overcame Tommy John surgery (2004) and shoulder surgery (2006) to reach the majors. His MLB career record was 0√¢¬Ä¬ì1 with a 4.00 ERA across 11 appearances. His greatest success came in the KBO League, where he compiled a 61√¢¬Ä¬ì37 record with a 3.66 ERA and 675 strikeouts over six seasons, highlighted by a league-leading 19 wins and the Golden Glove Award in 2015 with the NC Dinos. He stands 6'1", weighs 230 lbs, throws right-handed, and is a switch hitter. He currently lives in Southlake, Texas, with his wife Christine.
